title: "Issues with fglrx and ATI X850."
date: 2005-12-11
forum: Multimedia &amp; Video
---

### Post by okt on 2005-12-11
When trying to use the fglrx drivers with my card X fails to start.
Here are some logs and my xorg.conf. If you can see a problem please let me know.

**xorg.conf**
```

#    Chipset     "generic"

# The Driver line must be present.  When using run-time loadable driver

# module.  Even when not using loadable driver modules, this line

# === disable/enable XAA/DRI ===
    Option "no_accel"                   "no"
    Option "no_dri"                     "no"
# === misc DRI settings ===
    Option "mtrr"                       "off" # disable DRI mtrr mapper, driver has its own code for mtrr
# ### FireGL DDX driver module specific settings ###
# === Screen Management ===
    Option "DesktopSetup"               "(null)" 
    Option "HSync2"                     "unspecified" 
    Option "VRefresh2"                  "unspecified" 
    Option "ScreenOverlap"              "0" 
    Option "GammaCorrectionI"           "0x00000000"
    Option "GammaCorrectionII"          "0x00000000"
# === OpenGL specific profiles/settings ===
    Option "Capabilities"               "0x00000000"
    Option "CapabilitiesEx"             "0x00000000"
# === Video Overlay for the Xv extension ===
    Option "VideoOverlay"               "on"
# === OpenGL Overlay ===
# Note: When OpenGL Overlay is enabled, Video Overlay
#       will be disabled automatically
    Option "OpenGLOverlay"              "off"
# === Center Mode (Laptops only) ===
    Option "CenterMode"                 "off"
# === Pseudo Color Visuals (8-bit visuals) ===
    Option "PseudoColorVisuals"         "off"
# === QBS Management ===
    Option "Stereo"                     "off"
    Option "StereoSyncEnable"           "1"
# === FSAA Management ===
    Option "FSAAEnable"                 "no"
    Option "FSAAScale"                  "1"
    Option "FSAADisableGamma"           "no"
    Option "FSAACustomizeMSPos"         "no"
    Option "FSAAMSPosX0"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosY0"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosX1"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosY1"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosX2"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosY2"                "0.000000"

    Option "FSAAMSPosY3"                "0.000000"

    Option "FSAAMSPosY4"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosX5"                "0.000000"
    Option "FSAAMSPosY5"                "0.000000"
# === Misc Options ===
    Option "UseFastTLS"                 "0"
    Option "BlockSignalsOnLock"         "on"
    Option "UseInternalAGPGART"         "yes"
    Option "ForceGenericCPU"            "no"
#    BusID "PCI:1:0:0"    # no device found at config time
    Screen 0
EndSection

# **********************************************************************
# Screen sections
# **********************************************************************

# Any number of screen sections may be present.  Each describes
# the configuration of a single screen.  A single specific screen section
# may be specified from the X server command line with the "-screen"
# option.
Section "Screen"
    Identifier  "Screen0"
    Device      "ATI Graphics Adapter"
    Monitor     "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth 24
    #Option "backingstore"

    Subsection "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes       "800x600" "1024x768" "1280x1024" "1600x1200"
        ViewPort    0 0  # initial origin if mode is smaller than desktop
#        Virtual     1280 1024
    EndSubsection
EndSection

# **********************************************************************
# ServerLayout sections.
# **********************************************************************

# Any number of ServerLayout sections may be present.  Each describes
# the way multiple screens are organised.  A specific ServerLayout
# section may be specified from the X server command line with the
# "-layout" option.  In the absence of this, the first section is used.
# When now ServerLayout section is present, the first Screen section
# is used alone.

Section "ServerLayout"

# The Identifier line must be present
    Identifier  "Server Layout"

# Each Screen line specifies a Screen section name, and optionally
# the relative position of other screens.  The four names after
# primary screen name are the screens to the top, bottom, left and right
# of the primary screen.

    Screen "Screen0"

# Each InputDevice line specifies an InputDevice section name and
# optionally some options to specify the way the device is to be
# used.  Those options include "CorePointer", "CoreKeyboard" and
# "SendCoreEvents".

    InputDevice "Mouse1" "CorePointer"
    InputDevice "Keyboard1" "CoreKeyboard"

EndSection

### EOF ###

```

### Post by mlomker on 2005-12-11
I see a couple interesting things in the xorg.conf.  One is that it shows an on-board VGA controller.  Do you have one and are you 100% sure it is disabled in the BIOS?

The second thing is that your cards are being listed as unknown and the fglrxconfig doesn't recognize a card at all.

Try the following command to update the pciids and then post the output of the second command:

```

sudo update-pciids
lspci | grep VGA

```

The update is only a cosmetic thing but I'm curious why the driver cannot see that the card is installed.  This is from your kern.log:

```

[fglrx:firegl_init] *ERROR* Device not found!

```

---

### Post by okt on 2005-12-11
**"lspci | grep VGA" output**
```
0000:01:00.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc R480 [Radeon X850XT]
```


EDIT: Another strange thing is when I launch "ATi Control" i get:
"**Fire GL Control**"
"Driver does not provide the FireGL X11 extensions! Panel components will operate only partially."
Could this mean it is misreading my X850 for a FireGL?

---

### Post by haykel on 2005-12-11
try changing
```
Option "UseInternalAGPGART"         "yes"
```
to
```
Option "UseInternalAGPGART"         "no"
```

---

### Post by okt on 2005-12-11
@haykel, Nothing...

After running
```
fglrxconfig
```
again I was still unable to detect my card.

I am assuming that because it cant detect it then it isn't able to configure itself. Any ideas?

BTW I can discuss this over IRC, or IM. I can then post the logs and what not to assist other people who may encounter this problem.

---

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-12
Hmm... I'm actually thinking of getting this card soon... I hope you guys can figure out what's wrong :P.

The way I got my 3d accelleration working with my 9800pro was to recompile a new kernel following some settings I found here: [http://odin.prohosting.com/wedge01/gentoo-radeon-faq.html#kernel](http://odin.prohosting.com/wedge01/gentoo-radeon-faq.html#kernel)
To build the kernel, I used the instructions found here: [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelHowto](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelHowto)

After doing that, I installed the fglrx drivers using these instructions: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=423584](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=423584)
Note that I compiled my kernel with agp built in, so I made sure to note what was said here: [http://odin.prohosting.com/wedge01/gentoo-radeon-faq.html#3_howinst](http://odin.prohosting.com/wedge01/gentoo-radeon-faq.html#3_howinst) about whether to use the internal agp drivers or not.  In my case, since they were built into the kernel, I did not use the internal modules of the fglrx drivers, therefore I answered yes to "Do you want to use the external AGP GART module (y/n)?", which set the option "UseInternalAGPGART" in my xorg.conf file to "no".  The wording of that question needs to be fixed.. negative logic is confusing at times :P.  For the x850 I'm not sure if it will like using kernel modules, or the internal driver ones better... so I guess it's just trial and error.

EDIT: I just found a promising tutorial on getting the drivers to work with an x850xt!  It's not specifically for Ubuntu or Debian.. but it may help: [http://gentoo-wiki.com/HOWTO_X850XT_ATI_Drivers](http://gentoo-wiki.com/HOWTO_X850XT_ATI_Drivers)

---

### Post by okt on 2005-12-12
I am extreamly new to Linux, and after reading that I am still scratching my head. If someone could lend a hand in helping me with the hexediting part, and then doing that stuff with the kernel...

```

evan@ubuntu:~$ lspci -v | grep ATI
0000:01:00.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc R480 [Radeon X850XT] (prog-if 00 [VGA])
        Subsystem: ATI Technologies Inc: Unknown device 0312
0000:01:00.1 Display controller: ATI Technologies Inc R480 [Radeon X850XT secondary]
        Subsystem: ATI Technologies Inc: Unknown device 0313

```
So 1:0:0 is primary, and 1:0:1 is secondary?
```

evan@ubuntu:~$ lspci -n | grep 01:00.0
0000:01:00.0 0300: 1002:4b49

```
chip-id= 4b49

---

### Post by mlomker on 2005-12-12
[QUOTE=okt]
So 1:0:0 is primary, and 1:0:1 is secondary?
[/QUOTE]

Yes.  Now that I've looked on the [Rage3D forum]("http://www.rage3d.com/board/showthread.php?t=33834517"), I can see that it is a [known bug]("http://ati.cchtml.com/show_bug.cgi?id=236").

You'll have to either hex edit the driver (not sure what tool to use for that but I'd recommend posting on the Rage3D thread for help) or wait for the next release of the driver.

Regarding the other posts on this thread...I wish people would stop worrying about the gart.  Trying to disable the gart on a Ubuntu kernel doesn't do anything because it's built in and cannot be shut off.  I've also built my own kernel and didn't see any performance improvement by using ATI's gart.

---

### Post by mlomker on 2005-12-12
[QUOTE=okt]What file? I searched for flgrx.ko and came up with nothing.[/QUOTE]

Then you must not have gotten the driver properly installed.  Here's where it is on my machine:

```

/lib/modules/2.6.12-10-k7/misc/fglrx.ko

```

---

### Post by okt on 2005-12-12
[QUOTE=mlomker]Then you must not have gotten the driver properly installed.  Here's where it is on my machine:

```

/lib/modules/2.6.12-10-k7/misc/fglrx.ko

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-12
hmm... if you have a file called "libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3", that's the one it says to edit in the tutorial.  Find your listed chipid either in the 'supported' list in the tutorial, or probably a better idea (just in case the drivers have changed since that tutorial was made) is to actually search for the list in your own Xorg.0.log file.
To do this, execute:
```
grep -n "(II) ATI Radeon/FireGL: The following chipsets are supported:" /var/log/Xorg.0.log
```
You should get back something like:
```
308:(II) ATI Radeon/FireGL: The following chipsets are supported:
```
Note that line number Before the acutal match.  In my case it was line 308 in the Xorg.0.log file.  Now just open up the Xorg.0.log file in a text editor/viewer and goto that line number.  There should be a whole list of supported cards there.  Just find your card in the list, whether it's an x850 pro, xt, or Platinum Edition.  Note down the second number in the parentheses, ie: RADEON X850 XT (R480 **5D52**).  So if you were using the tutorial's list, you would use 5D52 as your search in the hexedit program.
Search for it in your hexedit program, and then replace with your real chipid that you found out earlier.  (also note, if you don't find 5D52 in the file, it may have been compiled in the opposite endian mode than normal... whatever is considered the normal one :p.  So instead, switch the 'D' and '2' and search for 525D.)

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-12
[QUOTE=okt]Ok I found that string. So I am to replace that with 4b49?[/QUOTE]
If you were able to find the 525D ("R]") instead of 5D52 ("]R"), then you also need to switch the order of your chipid.  So instead of 4B49 ("KI"), it would be 494B ("IK").

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-14
Ah... this could be your problem.  You seem to be using the pre-installed fglrx drivers included with the ubuntu linux-restricted-modules package.
You should get the new driver installer from ATI's site.  You should find it under Customer Care->Drivers & Software->Linux Display Drivers and Software.  Just choose the correct package for your cpu architecture (x86 or x86-64 bit), and click on the RADEON 8500 series or better link under the sub list for your processor.
You can follow the guide to installing these drivers here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=423584](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=423584)

Hope this helps

---

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-15
hmm... maybe you edited the wrong file then?
It seems strange for the file to be in the '' folder if it was the ATI driver.
Try following these commands and see if the file exists in this location:
```

   cd /lib/modules/fglrx/build_mod/                #cd to fglrx build-dir
   ls                                                        #list the files and look for libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3 here
   cp libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3 libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3_org    #make a backup (just in case it messes up)
   ghex2 libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3                     #open up the file with ghex and make your modifications

```

If this doesn't work, and your computer won't start x for some reason... then you can start up in recovery mode, and do the following:
```

cd /lib/modules/fglrx/build_mod/            #go back to the directory
rm libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3                          #remove the hex edited file (in recovery mode you should already be root, so no need for sudo)
mv libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3_org libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3     #rename the backup file back to the original name

```

If the file is not found i nthe /lib/modules/fglrx/build_mod/ directory, then I'm not sure where else to look... I'm just following the walkthrough here, so I'm not sure where the ATI installer actually installs to besides this folder.
Try searching again for it using: 
```

locate libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3

```

Note that you want to get the actual module file, and not the source.  So the path should probably have the word 'module' in it somewhere.

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-18
Hmm... it looks to be defaulting back to the original mesa drivers using that chipid.  Since you hexed the file, replacing 4a48 with 4b49, I wonder if using the chipid 0x4b49 would work?

From my understanding, the reason for hex editing the driver module file is to trick it into working on your chipid.  So... whatever it finds as your chipid, it tries to find this in the list of supported chips for the driver.  Since the driver does not officially support the x850 at the moment, you have to manually change the file so yours is detected as a supported one :p.

---

From my understanding, the reason for hex editing the driver module file is to trick it into working on your chipid.  So... whatever it finds as your chipid, it tries to find this in the list of supported chips for the driver.  Since the driver does not officially support the x850 at the moment, you have to manually change the file so yours is detected as a supported one :p.[/QUOTE]
If I try to use that id (4b49) X just fails to start with the no screens error.

### Post by TrinitronX on 2005-12-23
Ok... I've been looking at this for a while now, trying to figure out why it's not recognizing your card's chipid, and I think I may have some idea now.

If I understand now, you found the /lib/modules/fglrx/build_mod/libfglrx_ip.a.GCC3 file, and changed a **4a48** into a **4b49**.  So... this means you may have actually replaced the wrong hex value in the first place :p.  From earlier, you found out some info about your card with the following commands:
```

evan@ubuntu:~$ lspci -v | grep ATI
0000:01:00.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc R480 [Radeon X850XT] (prog-if 00 [VGA])
        Subsystem: ATI Technologies Inc: Unknown device 0312
0000:01:00.1 Display controller: ATI Technologies Inc R480 [Radeon X850XT secondary]
        Subsystem: ATI Technologies Inc: Unknown device 0313
evan@ubuntu:~$ lspci -n | grep 01:00.0
0000:01:00.0 0300: 1002:4b49

```

So your card is the Radeon X850XT with the R480 chip, which has a chipid of 4b49.  So... looking through the supported cards/chipid list that the ATI drivers put into the Xorg.0.log file, the chipid of **4a48** which you replaced actually cooresponds to the "RADEON X800 (R420 4A48)" card.  So... instead of replacing that chipid, you should be using the entry "RADEON X850 XT (R480 **5D52**)".

So... you should revert to your old backup file you made before hex editing, and try again, this time, searching for the **5D52** (or 525D), and replacing with your chipid of **4b49** (or 494b).

Also.. there may be one other problem here involving the new ati drivers, and having to edit another file.  I read through the whole tutorial a couple of times, and noticed this:
"Warning: For newer versions of ati driver you have to repeat the replace step in /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/drivers/fglrx_drv.o, because "ChipID" is not a option anymore and so the driver didn't recognize your card"

You may actually need to do this step too to get your card recognized by the drivers.  Just repeat the replacement, making sure to make a backup of the file beforehand.

After doing the hexediting, you need to do a bit of trickery to get them to re-install the new versions as kernel modules... so do the following:
```

   cd /lib/modules/fglrx/build_mod/
   rm ../fglrx.ko
   ./make.sh
   cd ..
   ./make_install.sh

```

This should remove the old fglrx.ko kernel module, and make/install a new one with your hex edited files.  After this is done, I'm assuming your shouldn't have to use the ChipID setting in your Xorg.conf file... since apparently it is not recognized as an option for the newer ATI drivers.

After recompiling the module, you need to load it into the kernel.  So... do the following (You may need to reboot and stop x by going into a lower runlevel, and then loading the module, and starting x again.):
```

sudo depmod
sudo modprobe fglrx

```

Hopefully everything will work at this point :p.

---
